title: CMMC Level 3 enhanced practice — threat scenario 5

compliance.assurance_rationale: CMMC AC.L2-3.1.1 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that CMMC AC.L2-3.1.1 (Authorized access to systems) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.20.15: CMMC Level 3 enhanced practice — threat scenario 5.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.20.15' running on WinEventLog:Security, CrowdStrike FDR, Defender for Office 365, NetFlow, encryption posture exports, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

description: Demonstrates contractor cybersecurity hygiene for "CMMC Level 3 enhanced practice — threat scenario 5" using CUI-scoped telemetry mapped to CMMC 2.0 Level 3 expectations.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for CMMC compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes vuln and sourcetypes tenable:was. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Confirm field extractions and CIM tags for the sourcetypes in scope; (2) Load or maintain the referenced lookups/KVStore collections with owner attestation dates; (3) Schedule the search at an interval aligned to the control’s materiality; (4) Route positive findings to the compliance ticketing queue with required evidence fields; (5) Retain scheduled search exports per records management and legal hold procedures. Indexes required: `index=vuln`.
visualization: Stacked bar, Table, Time chart, Single value. Key fields: `plugin_id`, `uc_tag`, `urls`.

grandmaExplanation: We watch for demonstrates contractor cybersecurity hygiene for "cmmc level 3 enhanced practice — threat scenario 5" using cui-scoped telemetry mapped to cmmc 2.0 level 3 expectations. This provides useful supporting evidence for your overall compliance programme.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for CrowdStrike FDR (5082), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Office 365 (4055), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621)..
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `WinEventLog:Security`, CrowdStrike FDR, Defender for Office 365, NetFlow, encryption posture exports.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Confirm field extractions and CIM tags for the sourcetypes in scope; (2) Load or maintain the referenced lookups/KVStore collections with owner attestation dates; (3) Schedule the search at an interval aligned to the control’s materiality; (4) Route positive findings to the compliance ticketing queue with required evidence fields; (5) Retain scheduled search exports per records management and legal hold procedures.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=vuln sourcetype=tenable:was earliest=-14d
| eval uc_tag="22.20.15"
| where scan_name="CUI-Web" AND risk="High"
| stats dc(url) as urls by plugin_id
```

Understanding this SPL

**CMMC Level 3 enhanced practice — threat scenario 5 (CMMC 2.0 Level 3)** — Demonstrates contractor cybersecurity hygiene for "CMMC Level 3 enhanced practice — threat scenario 5" using CUI-scoped telemetry mapped to CMMC 2.0 Level 3 expectations.

Documented **Data sources**: `WinEventLog:Security`, CrowdStrike FDR, Defender for Office 365, NetFlow, encryption posture exports. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for CrowdStrike FDR (5082), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Office 365 (4055), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: vuln; **sourcetype**: tenable:was. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=vuln, sourcetype=tenable:was, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **uc_tag** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where scan_name="CUI-Web" AND risk="High"` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by plugin_id** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes by Processes.process_name, Processes.user, Processes.dest | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**CMMC Level 3 enhanced practice — threat scenario 5 (CMMC 2.0 Level 3)** — Demonstrates contractor cybersecurity hygiene for "CMMC Level 3 enhanced practice — threat scenario 5" using CUI-scoped telemetry mapped to CMMC 2.0 Level 3 expectations.

Documented **Data sources**: `WinEventLog:Security`, CrowdStrike FDR, Defender for Office 365, NetFlow, encryption posture exports. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for CrowdStrike FDR (5082), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Office 365 (4055), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Endpoint.Processes` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Stacked bar, Table, Time chart, Single value
